title: From Random Setup to Consistent Trading: The Method That Really Helped Me Improve My Trading Skills (Free, No fluff)
author: Ok-Basil2753
selftext:
Recently, I've received many private messages asking how I learned to trade, so I wanted to share my experience here.

No courses, no paid communities, and no secret formulas. It's simply a method I've summarized after countless trials and failures that has proven truly effective.

Initially, I didn't have a perfect trading setup. I often studied charts on my laptop in cafes, my car, or anywhere I had time. The important thing wasn't the environment, but the practice.

The following methods eventually started to work:

1.Stop chasing highs and lows, start waiting

The biggest shift for me was realizing that most market fluctuations aren't worth trading. I used to blindly follow the crowd, entering the market immediately whenever I saw a "about to go up" trend. Now I wait for consolidation patterns, clear price levels, and confirmation signals. Missed trades are bad trades.

2. Focus on a Few Stocks

Instead of scanning hundreds of stocks, I started focusing on just a small watchlist. You gradually learn their movements, recognizing which are false breakouts and which are price holds. This level of familiarity is often underestimated.

3. Simple price levels, complex indicators

Support levels, resistance levels, and a few moving averages (5/13/34/55). That's it. Usually, when price and volume move in sync with these levels, that's sufficient. I no longer use excessive charts; things become much clearer.

4. Volume is more important than people think

A breakout without volume is just noise. Prices only truly rise when volume appears at the right time.

5. Risk management is crucial

It took me a long time to accept this. Even if your judgment is only 50% correct, you can still make money as long as your losses are small and profitable stocks continue to rise. Cut your losses promptly; don't blindly average down.

6. Track your trades

I started recording entry points, exit points, and the reasons for entry. Good and bad patterns quickly become apparent. Most people ignore this, causing their trading to stagnate.

I learn every day, but it's these records that led me from occasional profits to more consistent profitability.

Lately, I've been sharing my watchlist and trading strategies with a small group of people. Nothing special, just a way for us to exchange ideas and improve together.

If you feel your trading is aimless and disorganized, or you want a rational market discussion environment, or if you're currently feeling confused or unstable in your trading, don't worry, you're not alone. This is part of the trading process.

If you feel I need to add anything or can offer some advice, I'd love to hear your thoughts. Of course, I'm also willing to discuss my strategies with you so we can exit the market together sooner.
